steps.title: ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY (AKI)
steps.text: In an anuric patient with no dehydration or extra-renal fluid losses, what is the correct approach to fluid management?
steps.rationale: In the absence of fluid losses, fluid is restricted to replacing insensible losses only. Potassium-containing fluids must be avoided in anuric patients to prevent hyperkalaemia.

textChoices.text: Replace insensible losses only if needed
Add potassium to maintenance IV fluids

steps.title: ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY (AKI)
steps.text: Which of the following is contraindicated in a child with pulmonary oedema and oliguria/anuria?
steps.rationale: Giving fluids in this setting worsens pulmonary oedema. These patients are fluid-overloaded and need respiratory support, diuretics, or dialysis—not additional fluids.

steps.title: ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY (AKI)
steps.text: A child with AKI has serum potassium of 6.7 mmol/L despite medical treatment. What is the next best step?
steps.rationale: Refractory hyperkalaemia (>6.5 mmol/L) not responsive to medical treatment is an indication for dialysis, per the AEIOU criteria.

steps.title: ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY (AKI)
steps.text: After a diagnostic fluid challenge of 20 mL/kg in a child with suspected prerenal AKI, urine output improves. What does this suggest?
steps.rationale: Improvement in urine output following fluid resuscitation supports prerenal AKI due to hypovolemia or dehydration.

steps.title: ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY (AKI)
steps.text: Which of the following is not an indication for dialysis in AKI (based on AEIOU)?
steps.rationale: Dialysis is indicated for refractory hyperkalaemia, not when potassium is well controlled. The AEIOU indications only apply when conservative management fails.

textChoices.text: Fluid overload not responsive to diuretics

steps.title: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)
steps.text: Which of the following is true regarding sodium intake in children with CKD and hypertension?
steps.rationale: Sodium restriction helps manage oedema and hypertension. However, sodium should not be restricted in salt-losing conditions.

textChoices.text: Restrict salt intake in patients with hypertension and oedema
Encourage salt intake in all CKD patients
Add salt to meals to prevent dehydration
Restrict sodium only in salt-losing nephropathies

steps.title: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)
steps.text: What is the most appropriate dietary recommendation for a child with CKD and serum phosphate of 2.0 mmol/L and eGFR <70 ml/min/1.73m²?
steps.rationale: High phosphate levels in CKD contribute to bone disease. Reducing dietary phosphate is key to management.

steps.title: Nephrotic Syndrome
steps.text: A child with nephrotic syndrome presents with severe edema and critically low serum albumin. Urine output is adequate. What is the most appropriate next step?
steps.rationale: In symptomatic hypoalbuminemia with adequate urine output, IV albumin 0.5–1 g/kg slowly over 4 hours with frusemide midwaywith frusemide helps mobilize fluid and relieve symptoms. Frusemide is given halfway through the infusion to promote diuresis.

textChoices.text: Restrict fluids and monitor for 24 hours
Give oral albumin and wait for steroid effect

steps.title: GLOMERULONEPHRITIS / NEPHRITIC SYNDROME
steps.text: Which of the following is a key indication to initiate dialysis in a child with glomerulonephritis?
steps.rationale: Dialysis is indicated when there is severe fluid overload that is not responsive to diuretic therapy, to prevent complications such as pulmonary edema or hypertensive crisis.

textChoices.text: Severe fluid overload unresponsive to diuretics

steps.title: HYPERKALAEMIA
steps.text: A critically ill child presents with a serum potassium of 6.9 mmol/L from a finger-prick sample. What should be the next step before proceeding with treatment?
steps.rationale: Pseudohyperkalaemia due to haemolysis is common; confirmation using a non-haemolysed venous or arterial sample is essential to avoid unnecessary treatment.

textChoices.text: Immediately give insulin and glucose infusion
Repeat potassium test using a non-haemolysed venous or arterial sample

steps.title: HYPERNATRAEMIA
steps.text: What is the recommended maximum rate of sodium correction in chronic or severe hypernatraemia?
steps.rationale: To prevent cerebral oedema and neurologic injury, sodium levels should not fall faster than 0.5 mEq/L/hour (10–12 mEq/L/day).

steps.title: HYPONATREMIA
steps.text: In a child presenting with severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (e.g. seizures or coma), what is the correct initial fluid of choice?
steps.rationale: Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia requires careful correction with hypertonic saline to prevent neurological complications like cerebral oedema.

steps.title: HYPOCALCAEMIA
steps.text: Which of the following is the gold standard for assessing calcium status in the presence of hypoalbuminemia?
steps.rationale: Ionized (free) calcium reflects the biologically active form and is not affected by albumin levels. It is the gold standard in assessing calcium status, especially in hypoalbuminemia.

steps.title: RENAL REPLACEMENT THERAPY (RRT)
steps.text: A child on peritoneal dialysis presents with signs of peritonitis. There are no systemic signs of infection. What is the most appropriate initial step?
steps.rationale: In the absence of systemic signs, empiric intraperitoneal antibiotics should be started promptly, and nephrology should be consulted.

textChoices.text: Delay antibiotics until culture results return
Initiate empiric intraperitoneal antibiotics and consult nephrology

steps.title: Acute severe hypertension
steps.text: In a child with hypertensive emergency, what is the initial target for systolic blood pressure control during IV antihypertensive treatment?
steps.rationale: The target in hypertensive emergency is to carefully reduce systolic BP to the 95th percentile for age and sex to avoid hypoperfusion-related complications.

textChoices.text: Below the 90th percentile for age and sex
At the 95th percentile for age and sex
Return to normal adult values within 2 hours
